translation: By the sky and the night comer
commentary:
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلطَّارِقِ

Allah ﷻ begins this surah with a powerful oath: "By the sky and the night visitor." This opening connects directly to the previous surah, Al-Buruj, which also began with an oath by the sky. While Al-Buruj addressed the accountability of nations and powerful oppressors, At-Tariq shifts focus to individual accountability. Each of us has our own sphere of influence—whether as a head of household, a manager, a teacher, or even an older sibling. In these small worlds, we too exercise authority and bear responsibility for our actions.

The word الطَّارِق carries rich meaning in Arabic. It refers to someone who arrives at night and announces their presence by knocking—specifically, in the Arabian context, by striking rocks together outside a tent. This nighttime visitor could be understood as the shooting stars that traverse the sky, or perhaps the angels who visit in the darkness, including Angel Jibreel (AS) who came to the Prophet ﷺ during the night.

translation: And what can make you know what is the night comer
commentary:
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلطَّارِقُ

"And what can make you know what the night visitor is?" This rhetorical question builds anticipation and draws our attention to something extraordinary. Allah ﷻ deliberately leaves certain details unspecified—not as an oversight, but as a teaching method. When He wants us to focus on specific lessons, He withholds information that might distract us. Our obsession with details He didn't mention often comes at the expense of understanding what He actually did say.

ٱلنَّجْمُ ٱلثَّاقِبُ

Allah ﷻ answers His own question: "The piercing star." The word الثَّاقِبُ means something that pierces through with force. The Arabs, who spent countless clear desert nights observing the sky, could distinguish between different stars. Some scholars suggested this refers to Saturn, whose light appeared most piercing to the naked eye. However, Allah ﷻ left the specific identity open-ended for a reason.

The concept of piercing appears elsewhere in the Quran, particularly regarding the shooting stars that strike down jinn who attempt to steal information from the heavens. This creates a mental connection: just as these stars serve as defensive installations protecting the sanctity of the heavens, they also represent watchful guardians observing all that occurs on earth. The twinkling star reaching us from vast distances symbolizes the angels whose gaze pierces through space to monitor our actions.

There's also a beautiful parallel here with the Quran itself. Just as starlight pierces through darkness to reach us, the revelation of the Quran pierced through the darkness of ignorance to illuminate humanity. The stars serve as navigation tools for desert travelers at night; similarly, the ayat of the Quran guide us through the darkness of this world.

translation: There is no soul but that it has over it a protector
commentary:
إِن كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَّمَّا عَلَيْهَا حَافِظٌ

"Indeed, every soul has over it a guardian." This ayah can be read in two grammatically valid ways, both conveying the same essential meaning with different emphases. The word لَّمَّا serves as an emphatic particle, stressing that without any doubt, every single person has a حَافِظ—a guardian who both protects and records.

This guardian serves multiple functions. On one hand, angels protect us constantly. When you drive safely, when accidents are narrowly avoided, when danger passes you by—these are often the unseen workings of guardian angels. But the word حَافِظ also carries the connotation of a watchful guard who restrains and records. These same angels who protect us also document our every deed.

The root of حَافِظ relates to constancy and reliability, like a road that remains safe and passable at all times. These guardians never sleep, never take breaks, never fail in their vigilance. They are perpetually alert, recording everything.

The very fact that we're being recorded carries profound implications. If there's a record being kept, it will eventually be examined. If evidence is being collected, it will be presented. The institution of guardianship over us necessarily points to a coming Day of Judgment when this record will be opened and reviewed. Allah ﷻ doesn't need to explicitly mention the afterlife here—the concept of constant surveillance and recording logically demands it.

translation: So let man observe from what he was created
commentary:
فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ

"So let man observe from what he was created." Here lies a profound irony. We've just learned about the elaborate system watching over us—stars in the heavens, guardian angels assigned to each soul. We must be tremendously important creatures to warrant such attention. But now Allah ﷻ tells us to examine our actual origins.

This instruction to reflect serves multiple purposes. Yes, it reminds us that the One who created us initially can certainly recreate us for resurrection. But in the context of this surah, there's a more immediate point: Look at the contrast between the grand measures taken to watch over you and the humble reality of what you actually are.

translation: He was created from a fluid, ejected
commentary:
خُلِقَ مِن مَّآءٍ دَافِقٍ

"He was created from a fluid, ejected." The word دَافِق describes something that gushes or springs forth forcefully. This is our origin—a drop of fluid so insignificant that people wash it off their clothes without a second thought. 

Here's the humbling reality: So much divine attention, such elaborate systems of surveillance and protection, all for a creature whose beginning was this humble fluid. This stark contrast should inspire both humility and gratitude. We are, in our physical essence, nothing special. The honor bestowed upon us comes entirely from Allah ﷻ. When the Quran says "We have honored the children of Adam," this is what it means—Allah ﷻ took something with such lowly origins and elevated it, breathed into it divine light, granted it dignity and purpose. Without that divine light, we would be no different from animals.

translation: Emerging from between the backbone and the ribs
commentary:
يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ ٱلصُّلْبِ وَٱلتَّرَآئِبِ

"Emerging from between the backbone and the ribs." The most compelling understanding of this ayah relates to the child emerging from the mother's body. During pregnancy and delivery, the baby presses against the mother's ribs and puts tremendous pressure on her back. The journey into this world involves a gush of fluid entering the mother, and a gush of fluid—mixed with blood—as the baby emerges. This is hardly a dignified entrance into existence.

The word الصُّلْبِ refers to the backbone, the hardest and strongest part of the human body. التَّرَآئِبِ refers to the ribs, which by comparison are softer and more fragile. This pairing of hard and soft creates a beautiful parallel with the earth itself. The earth has hard surfaces and soft soil, and from between these, life springs forth when rain falls. Similarly, from between the hard backbone and soft ribs of the mother, new human life emerges.

This imagery prepares us for what comes later in the surah—the mention of the sky that brings rain and the earth that splits open. Just as water penetrates the earth and produces diverse forms of life, so too does that original fluid create human life. And just as rain will one day fall to resurrect the dead from hard and soft earth, Allah ﷻ is fully capable of bringing us back to life.

translation: Indeed, Allah, to return him [to life], is Able
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَجْعِهِۦ لَقَادِرٌ

"Indeed, He is able to return him." The One who orchestrated your entire creation from a single drop, who guided your formation in your mother's womb, who brought you forth into this world—why would recreating you be difficult? Your very existence is evidence of Allah's power to resurrect you. 

The word رَجْع also carries the meaning of summoning someone back for accountability. You're not just being brought back to life; you're being summoned to answer for the record that those guardian angels have been keeping.

translation: The Day when secrets will be put on trial
commentary:
يَوْمَ تُبْلَى ٱلسَّرَآئِرُ

"On the Day when secrets will be put to trial." The word تُبْلَى comes from a harsh root. It originally described the cruel practice of tying an enemy's camel to their grave and leaving it to die of starvation—testing the absolute limits of the animal's endurance. From this came the meaning of testing something to its maximum capacity, stretching it to its breaking point.

On the Day of Judgment, all the secrets held within hearts will be tested to their limits. Every hidden intention, every concealed motive, every private thought—all will be extracted, examined, and evaluated. The strength of your faith, the purity of your intentions, the sincerity of your worship—all will be measured precisely.

This is crucial to understand: Allah ﷻ doesn't just evaluate our outward deeds. He examines what lies beneath them. Consider this—we all came here to pray, but some prayed with hearts full of consciousness of Allah ﷻ, while others merely went through the motions. Some said "As-salamu alaykum" with genuine love for their brother, while others said it while harboring resentment in their hearts. We might perform the same actions outwardly, but the secrets within make all the difference.

The story of the Buddhist man who attended the masjid for the first time illustrates this beautifully. He knew nothing of Islamic prayer. When he understood that Allah ﷻ cannot be limited to a statue, he was so overwhelmed that he prostrated immediately—hands in the wrong place, facing the wrong direction, everything technically incorrect. Yet the sincerity and awe in his heart at that moment was genuine submission to Allah ﷻ. When the magicians of Pharaoh fell into prostration before Allah ﷻ, they didn't consult about the proper fiqh position—they were overwhelmed by the truth. That's what will be tested: the reality of what was in the heart.

I'm not dismissing the importance of proper ritual worship. The specific forms of our prayers beautify our religion and perfect it. But the heart of the matter—literally—is the condition of the heart itself. The outward forms without the inward reality are empty shells. On the other hand, focusing only on the spiritual while dismissing the divine instructions is equally misguided. Both matter, but never forget that on the Day of Judgment, يَوْمَ تُبْلَى ٱلسَّرَآئِرُ—the secrets will be tested.

translation: Then man will have no power or any helper
commentary:
فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن قُوَّةٍ وَلَا نَاصِرٍ

"Then he will have no power, nor any helper." The word قُوَّة comes from a root meaning to break through something—it describes strength measured by the ability to pierce through obstacles. On that Day, you won't have the strength to break out of your situation, to escape your accountability.

The word نَاصِر (helper) is equally profound. It comes from a root related to pathways that channel water to valleys—routes of salvation or escape. In military terms, it refers to reinforcements that arrive to save an army from annihilation. It's the aid that comes when all seems lost.

On the Day of Judgment, you'll have neither offense nor defense. No power to break free, no helper to plead your case or rescue you from the consequences of your deeds. This completes the image that began with guardian angels watching over us—the record they kept will now be presented, examined, and judged, with no possibility of escape or intervention.

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلرَّجْعِ

The second half of the surah begins, mirroring the opening with another oath: "By the sky which returns." The word الرَّجْعِ refers to rain that comes again and again, reliably returning at its appointed times. Every time rain falls, it brings the earth back to life.

This oath presents both aspects of the sky that we need to understand. The first oath by the sky emphasized surveillance—the stars watching over us. This second oath emphasizes resurrection—the rain that repeatedly revives dead earth. Together, these two concepts complete our understanding of accountability: we are being watched, and we will be brought back.

translation: And [by] the earth which cracks open
commentary:
وَٱلْأَرْضِ ذَاتِ ٱلصَّدْعِ

"And by the earth which splits open." When rain penetrates the earth, the ground cracks and splits, allowing seeds to sprout. Water affects the earth in various ways: sometimes it creates puddles where animals drink, sometimes it forms wells or rivers, sometimes it penetrates deep to nourish seeds. And sometimes water simply runs off, leaving the land barren.

This parallels exactly how revelation affects human hearts. Some people absorb the Quran's guidance and blossom with faith and good deeds. Others acquire knowledge of revelation but fail to benefit personally, though others may benefit from them. Still others remain completely unaffected—the revelation slides right off them like water off hardened ground.

The connection to pregnancy and birth becomes even clearer here. Just as a child emerges from between the mother's hard backbone and soft ribs, plants emerge from between the hard rocks and soft soil of the earth. Just as rain brings forth diverse forms of plant life from the same earth, revelation brings forth unique forms of goodness from different believers. Look at the companions of the Prophet ﷺ—though they all received the same revelation, each manifested its teachings differently according to their distinct capabilities and personalities. Like plants that taste different depending on the soil and climate, human goodness takes diverse, beautiful forms under the nourishment of divine guidance.

translation: Indeed, the Qur'an is a decisive statement
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ

"Indeed, it is a decisive statement." After swearing by the sky that brings rain and the earth that splits open, Allah ﷻ speaks about the Quran itself. Just as rain benefits the earth, revelation benefits humanity. Just as rain revives dead earth, the Quran revives dead hearts. Just as water extracts the hidden potential in seeds, revelation extracts the goodness hidden within human beings.

The word فَصْل comes from the concept of separation. Imagine pearls on a necklace with beads between them creating gaps—those gaps allow you to distinguish one pearl from another. فَصْل means creating clear separation so things can be distinguished. The Quran is فَصْل—it definitively separates truth from falsehood, right from wrong, guidance from misguidance, acceptable from unacceptable.

When Allah ﷻ uses the intensive form قَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ, He's saying this is the very definition of decisive speech. Nothing is ambiguous, nothing is mixed or confused. For things to be clear, they must be separated; for things to be confusing, they must be mixed together. The Quran achieves perfect clarity by making perfect distinctions.

وَمَا هُوَ بِٱلْهَزْلِ

"And it is not amusement." Consider the irony of this statement's timing. When this was revealed in Makkah, the Prophet ﷺ held no political power, no social influence, no economic advantage. By worldly measures, he was in a position of weakness—at best ridiculed, at worst persecuted. Yet from that position of apparent powerlessness, Allah ﷻ describes the words coming through him as incredibly powerful, absolutely serious, not weak in the slightest.

The word هَزْل means something weak, flimsy, not well-nourished—like a malnourished baby desperately needing to feed. This creates a striking contrast: human beings emerged from that pathetic fluid, weak and needy. But the Quran? وَمَا هُوَ بِٱلْهَزْلِ—it's not weak at all. It's robust, powerful, nourishing rather than needing nourishment.

You are weak. Revelation is powerful. Hold onto Allah's book and you gain strength. Let it go, and you remain in your natural state of weakness. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere, "They have not appraised Allah with true appraisal. Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might." Human beings are weak—Allah ﷻ is Mighty. Connect yourself to His word and His might becomes your source of strength.

translation: Indeed, they are planning a plan
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ يَكِيدُونَ كَيْدًا

"Indeed, they are planning a plan." This serves as consolation to the Prophet ﷺ. They are definitely scheming against you—there's no doubt about that. Whether you know the details of their plots or not, understand one thing with certainty: the plotting and scheming is happening.

But here's a profound point: why would they need to scheme if this message were truly weak? You don't plot and plan against something powerless. You only make elaborate schemes when you acknowledge—even if only privately—that something poses a real threat. The very fact that they're scheming proves the power of what they claim to oppose.

Their statements that the Quran is weak, that it's just folklore, that it's unimpressive—these are themselves part of their scheme. When people feel truly intimidated, they often attack with dismissive labels rather than substantive arguments. "The Quran is just a hodgepodge of statements," they say, while it transforms the world. Let them make these claims. Their scheming itself testifies to their fear of the truth.

translation: But I am planning a plan
commentary:
وَأَكِيدُ كَيْدًا

"And I am planning a plan." Notice the contrast in emphasis. They are exhausting themselves making scheme after scheme—different groups with different plots, all working to destroy the message. And Allah's response? Simple, understated, effortless: "And I am planning a plan." One divine plan effortlessly undoes all their countless schemes.

This should liberate us from obsession with conspiracy theories. Since I became conscious of Islam, Muslims have been constantly anxious about plots against us. We see schemes everywhere, convinced someone is always planning our destruction. And yes, opposition to truth is real. But look at the Quranic response: acknowledge their scheming exists, then trust in Allah's superior plan. Don't waste your energy worrying about their plots—focus on doing what you need to do. Responding to their schemes is Allah's responsibility, not yours.

translation: So allow time for the disbelievers. Leave them awhile
commentary:
فَمَهِّلِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْهِلْهُمْ رُوَيْدًۢا

"So allow time for the disbelievers. Leave them alone for a little while." This is where the surah reaches its remarkable conclusion. مَهِّل means to take it easy, to not rush. Allah ﷻ is telling His Messenger ﷺ: go easy on them. Don't be hasty in praying against them. Don't let your anger consume you.

This is profound when you consider that the Prophet ﷺ had been given the stories of previous prophets. He knew how Nuh (AS) finally said, "My Lord, do not leave upon the earth any of the disbelievers." He knew how other prophets reached their breaking point. The Prophet ﷺ could have used those same words—and if he had, the consequences would have been immediate and devastating.

But Allah ﷻ says: No, you're too powerful for that. Have mercy on these fools. They don't understand what they're doing. You're not the victim here—they will be if you speak a word against them. In the previous surah, when Allah ﷻ described His powerful grip, He then told His Messenger ﷺ: "Forgive." That theme continues here with even more emphasis.

Then Allah ﷻ adds أَمْهِلْهُمْ—give them time. Make it a policy, not a case-by-case decision. Don't keep a running list of grievances waiting for the final straw. Just let it be your approach: patience, always patience.

Finally, رُوَيْدًۢا—just a little while longer. It won't be forever. The word comes from the concept of slowly melting metal—you can't rush it, it takes time. Let them dig their own hole. Let them continue. Allah's plan is already in motion. You don't need to worry about vengeance—He will handle justice. Your role is simply to be patient, to go easy on them, to give them time.

This instruction hints at what's coming: soon the Prophet ﷺ would leave Makkah for Madinah. Soon the doors of revelation would close for those who rejected it. Soon the promises would be fulfilled. But for now? Right now, the command is simple: take it easy, be patient, trust in Allah's plan.

---

The surah's structure is remarkable in its symmetry. It begins with an oath by the sky and ends with another oath by the sky. Between these bookends, everything appears in pairs—matching the theme of duality woven throughout. The first half establishes that we're being watched and we're weak. The second half establishes that the Quran is decisive and powerful, they are scheming but Allah ﷻ has His plan, and ultimately we should be patient.

Just as the sky "returns" (الرَّجْعِ), the surah itself returns to its opening themes, rebuilding the argument in perfect parallel. This is the signature style of At-Tariq—everything doubled, everything reflected, everything brought back around. It's a stunning example of how Allah's perfect word manifests not just in meaning but in structure, in the very order and rhythm of divine speech.

May Allah ﷻ grant us deep understanding of His book and allow us to live by its guidance.
